title: Debugging Node.js Applications
description: Use console.log, debugger, and Node's built-in inspector.

slides.title: Console.Log Debugging 🐞
slides.content:
# The Simplest Debugger

```javascript
console.log('Value of x:', x);
console.log('Reached this point');
```

Pros: quick, no setup. Cons: clutter, need to remove later.

You can use `console.dir(obj, { depth: null })` to print nested objects.

slides.title: Using the Node.js Debugger 🔍
slides.content:
Node has a built-in debugger. Insert `debugger;` statement in your code, then run:
```bash
node inspect myscript.js
```

Then you can use commands like `cont`, `next`, `step`, `repl` to inspect variables.

Alternatively, use Chrome DevTools: run with `--inspect` flag and open `chrome://inspect`.

slides.title: VS Code Debugging 🖥️
slides.content:
Visual Studio Code has excellent Node debugging.

1. Open your project.
2. Go to Run and Debug view.
3. Create a `launch.json` configuration or just click "Run and Debug" and choose Node.js.
4. Set breakpoints by clicking in the gutter.
5. Press F5 to start debugging.

You can inspect variables, watch expressions, and step through code.

slides.title: Using the `util` Module for Inspection 🧰

quizData.question: What flag runs Node with the built-in inspector, allowing Chrome DevTools to connect?
quizData.explanation: `--inspect` enables the inspector; you can then open `chrome://inspect` to attach DevTools.
